title: Composition of functions
short: Feeding one function's output into another: (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x)).
full:
Composing two functions means applying one after the other: (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x)) reads 'do g first, then f'. The output of g becomes the input of f.

Order matters — f ∘ g is usually different from g ∘ f. The domain of f ∘ g is restricted to inputs that g can accept and whose g-values lie in the domain of f. Composition is how complicated functions are built from simple ones, and recognising a composition is the first step of the chain rule in calculus.
